questions.text: I maintain an archive of past work so I can reference and build on it.
I feel that my emotional reactions are like weather systems — powerful, fast-moving, and hard to control.
I tend not to detect when a story is being told for the benefit of a specific listener.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering reveals the depth of my values in a way that ordinary life does not.
I believe that openness to others' views is necessary for effective teamwork.
I notice when someone breaks eye contact at a telling moment.
I believe that self-discipline compounds over time into extraordinary results.
I hold the view that the obligation to respond to suffering is among the most non-negotiable commitments a person can hold.
I feel that my emotional system runs with a kind of quiet efficiency.
I notice the way someone's tone shifts slightly when they mention a particular person.
I value speed and momentum over precision in the early stages of any effort.
I feel most proud of the things I did for others, not the things I achieved for myself.
I think that approaching others' distress with calm, logical analysis is the behavior that most reliably converts good intentions into measurable improvements.
I believe that making others' lives easier is more rewarding than personal achievement.
I feel things so deeply that it can be physically exhausting.
I hold the view that self-care is not indulgence—it is strategy.
I feel restless when no one is taking charge of a situation.
I feel confident making decisions on behalf of a group.
